context:
Page 5, Section 1.5 (Extensions)

Whether this can also be achieved with static prices remains open, just as the question of whether a double-exponentially fast approach is possible with dynamic prices.
title: Achieve doubly-exponential competition complexity using dynamic prices in combinatorial auctions
background_latex:
Consider a combinatorial auction with a finite set of items \,\(M\) and \,\(n\) agents arriving online in a known order \,\(1,2,\dots,n\). Each agent \,\(i\in[n]\) has a valuation function \,\(v_i:2^M\to\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\), drawn independently from a known distribution \,\(F_i\) over a class of valuations (e.g., XOS or submodular). An online posted-pricing policy posts item prices and agents purchase bundles that maximize their quasilinear utility.

A (possibly adaptive) pricing policy specifies, before each agent arrives, a nonnegative price \,\(p_{i,j}\ge 0\) for each currently unsold item \,\(j\in M\). Upon arrival, agent \,\(i\) observes current prices and chooses a bundle \,\(T_i\subseteq M_i\) of currently available items \,\(M_i\subseteq M\) that maximizes
\[
T_i \in \arg\max_{T\subseteq M_i}\bigl(v_i(T)-\sum_{j\in T} p_{i,j}\bigr).
\]
Ties may be broken arbitrarily. Items purchased are removed from availability.

We consider a resource-augmentation (repetition) model with \,\(k\) independent copies (“blocks”) of buyers. In block \,\(b\in[k]\), there is one copy of each agent type \,\(i\in[n]\) with valuation \,\(v_i^{(b)}\sim F_i\) independently across \,\(i\) and \,\(b\). The pricing policy observes the sequence of arriving buyers across all \,\(nk\) arrivals and may update prices after each purchase (dynamic prices). Let \,\(\mathrm{SW}_k\) denote the social welfare achieved by the policy on the \,\(k\)-block instance:
\[
\mathrm{SW}_k = \sum_{b=1}^k\sum_{i=1}^n v_i^{(b)}\bigl(T_i^{(b)}\bigr).
\]
On a single block (one copy of each agent), define the offline optimal welfare
\[
\mathrm{OPT}_1(v)=\max_{(S_1,\dots,S_n)\in\mathcal{P}(M)}\sum_{i=1}^n v_i(S_i),
\]
where \,\(\mathcal{P}(M)\) is the set of partitions of \,\(M\) into \,\(n\) (possibly empty) bundles.

For \,\(\varepsilon\in(0,1)\), the \((1-\varepsilon)\)-competition complexity of a class of pricing policies is the smallest \,\(k\) such that for every such product distribution \,\(F=F_1\times\cdots\times F_n\), there exists a policy in the class whose expected welfare on \,\(k\) blocks is at least \,\((1-\varepsilon)\) times the expected offline optimum on one block:
\[
\mathbb{E}_{v\sim F^k}[\mathrm{SW}_k]\;\ge\;(1-\varepsilon)\,\mathbb{E}_{v\sim F}[\mathrm{OPT}_1(v)].
\]
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether, for submodular or XOS combinatorial auctions with independent (product) valuation distributions \,\(F=\times_{i=1}^n F_i\), there exists a class of fully dynamic posted-pricing policies whose \((1-\varepsilon)\)-competition complexity scales as 
\[
\Theta(\log\log(1/\varepsilon))
\]
(i.e., the number of blocks \,\(k\) needed to guarantee
\(\mathbb{E}_{v\sim F^k}[\mathrm{SW}_k]\ge (1-\varepsilon)\,\mathbb{E}_{v\sim F}[\mathrm{OPT}_1(v)]\)
can be taken proportional to \,\(\log\log(1/\varepsilon)\)).
latex:
Consider a combinatorial auction with a finite set of items \,\(M\) and \,\(n\) agents arriving online in a known order \,\(1,2,\dots,n\). Each agent \,\(i\in[n]\) has a valuation function \,\(v_i:2^M\to\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\), drawn independently from a known distribution \,\(F_i\) over a class of valuations (e.g., XOS or submodular). An online posted-pricing policy posts item prices and agents purchase bundles that maximize their quasilinear utility.

A (possibly adaptive) pricing policy specifies, before each agent arrives, a nonnegative price \,\(p_{i,j}\ge 0\) for each currently unsold item \,\(j\in M\). Upon arrival, agent \,\(i\) observes current prices and chooses a bundle \,\(T_i\subseteq M_i\) of currently available items \,\(M_i\subseteq M\) that maximizes
\[
T_i \in \arg\max_{T\subseteq M_i}\bigl(v_i(T)-\sum_{j\in T} p_{i,j}\bigr).
\]
Ties may be broken arbitrarily. Items purchased are removed from availability.

We consider a resource-augmentation (repetition) model with \,\(k\) independent copies (“blocks”) of buyers. In block \,\(b\in[k]\), there is one copy of each agent type \,\(i\in[n]\) with valuation \,\(v_i^{(b)}\sim F_i\) independently across \,\(i\) and \,\(b\). The pricing policy observes the sequence of arriving buyers across all \,\(nk\) arrivals and may update prices after each purchase (dynamic prices). Let \,\(\mathrm{SW}_k\) denote the social welfare achieved by the policy on the \,\(k\)-block instance:
\[
\mathrm{SW}_k = \sum_{b=1}^k\sum_{i=1}^n v_i^{(b)}\bigl(T_i^{(b)}\bigr).
\]
On a single block (one copy of each agent), define the offline optimal welfare
\[
\mathrm{OPT}_1(v)=\max_{(S_1,\dots,S_n)\in\mathcal{P}(M)}\sum_{i=1}^n v_i(S_i),
\]
where \,\(\mathcal{P}(M)\) is the set of partitions of \,\(M\) into \,\(n\) (possibly empty) bundles.

For \,\(\varepsilon\in(0,1)\), the \((1-\varepsilon)\)-competition complexity of a class of pricing policies is the smallest \,\(k\) such that for every such product distribution \,\(F=F_1\times\cdots\times F_n\), there exists a policy in the class whose expected welfare on \,\(k\) blocks is at least \,\((1-\varepsilon)\) times the expected offline optimum on one block:
\[
\mathbb{E}_{v\sim F^k}[\mathrm{SW}_k]\;\ge\;(1-\varepsilon)\,\mathbb{E}_{v\sim F}[\mathrm{OPT}_1(v)].
\]

Determine whether, for submodular or XOS combinatorial auctions with independent (product) valuation distributions \,\(F=\times_{i=1}^n F_i\), there exists a class of fully dynamic posted-pricing policies whose \((1-\varepsilon)\)-competition complexity scales as 
\[
\Theta(\log\log(1/\varepsilon))
\]
(i.e., the number of blocks \,\(k\) needed to guarantee
\(\mathbb{E}_{v\sim F^k}[\mathrm{SW}_k]\ge (1-\varepsilon)\,\mathbb{E}_{v\sim F}[\mathrm{OPT}_1(v)]\)
can be taken proportional to \,\(\log\log(1/\varepsilon)\)).
open_problem_quote: Whether this can also be achieved with static prices remains open, just as the question of whether a double-exponentially fast approach is possible with dynamic prices.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can computationally test candidate dynamic pricing policies on sampled valuation distributions and small item/agent instances to estimate achieved welfare ratios, but worst-case (over all product distributions) behavior is hard to certify by finite search alone. Counterexamples might be discoverable by automated search over small discrete-support distributions, yet translating them into general impossibility would still require proof.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Dynamic pricing policies admit a programmable representation (e.g., state-dependent price-update rules), and a fitness signal can be computed by Monte Carlo estimation of the welfare ratio on families of synthetic product distributions and small markets. An evolutionary loop could search for pricing rules that empirically achieve near-optimal ratios with small k (suggesting improved upper bounds) or for hard distributions that degrade known policy classes (suggesting separations).

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The forward-citation list was not provided, so the review is necessarily grounded in the source paper and its cited prior work. The source establishes doubly-exponential convergence in the single-item prophet inequality via block thresholds, and gives only preliminary combinatorial-auction extensions: block-consistent prices achieve O(\log(1/ε)) competition complexity for XOS/submodular valuations, while static prices require O(1/ε). The paper explicitly leaves open whether fully dynamic posted prices can improve this to Θ(\log\log(1/ε)) in combinatorial auctions.

Based on the current information, the problem remains open. Progress likely requires new structural characterizations of optimal dynamic pricing in combinatorial auctions (analogous to the block-threshold optimality in the single-item case) or new stochastic-dominance/duality arguments that can exploit adaptivity across blocks while respecting item competition and complement-free valuations.

sources.title: The Competition Complexity of Prophet Inequalities
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces the (1-ε)-competition complexity benchmark for prophet inequalities under resource augmentation. Proves Θ(\log\log(1/ε)) blocks suffice for single-item prophet inequalities via block thresholds, but for XOS/submodular combinatorial auctions only achieves O(\log(1/ε)) with block-consistent prices and O(1/ε) with static prices; explicitly leaves open whether fully dynamic posted prices can achieve Θ(\log\log(1/ε)) in combinatorial auctions.
sources.connection: This is the source paper posing the open problem: whether dynamic posted-pricing in XOS/submodular combinatorial auctions can match the doubly-exponential convergence (competition complexity Θ(\log\log(1/ε))) known for the single-item prophet setting.
